commentary: New year in the shop. Cleaned the bench, sorted the stone tray, sharpened the gravers. Sterling cuff on the block first thing tomorrow.
Finished a row of stamped bracelets this week. Same five stamps my grandfather used. They wear in over the years, which is the point.
Talked to a ninth-grade class about what the sheriff's office actually does day to day. Mostly listening, paperwork, and knowing your neighbors. Less like television.
Soldered for six hours and have one clean bezel to show for it. Some days the metal wins.
Community meeting at the chapter house tonight. Good turnout. Most of policing here is built before anything goes wrong, not after.
Picked up a tray of Kingman turquoise from Rio Grande this week. Good color, fair price. Cabs cut clean.
Set a stone in a ring for a customer's anniversary. Their grandmother's turquoise, reset into something they can wear daily. That is the work I like best.
Twenty-two years at the office this spring. Sixteen of them as a detective. Still learning the county.
Repair season. Three broken clasps and a snapped bolo tip on the bench. People keep their good pieces for decades, which I respect.
Drove out to Crownpoint for the rug auction. Did not buy. Came home with ideas for a stamp pattern instead.
Helped coach Kai's cross-country team set up the home meet. Cold morning, fast course. Good kids.
Shop is closed between Christmas and New Year. Catching up on orders by hand. No machines, no rush.
Annealed and forged a wide cuff today. The hammer marks are the finish. I do not polish those out.
Spent the morning with the youth mentoring program. A few of these kids just need one adult who shows up twice. That is most of it.
Bench tip: keep your flux fresh and your firebrick clean. Half of bad solder joints start before the torch ever lights.
Took a table at the Red Rock fall show. Sold a few cuffs, talked to more makers than buyers, which suits me fine.
Reminder for folks heading into the canyons this season: tell someone your route and when you will be back. Most of the calls we work start with nobody knowing where to look.
Made a batch of simple silver bands for the holiday table at the gallery. Nothing fancy. Sometimes a plain band is the whole job.
Quiet week. Rebuilt my pickle pot setup and reorganized the solder station. Boring jobs make the good ones go faster.
Thomas Kee came through the shop and looked over my stamp work. Forty years at the bench and he still found two things to fix. That is how you know a good mentor.
Ram's head cuff off the bench today. Kingman turquoise host, hand-stamped edges. Photos to follow. Turquoise Trail Gallery has first look.
My daughter Kai turned sixteen yesterday. Golden Dragon and slow driving lessons and everyone at the table pretended not to notice her spring in her step.
Long day on the caseload. Long evenings are for polishing bezels. Recommend both, in that order.
Forty-eight. Grateful for the work, the people, and one hell of an Aussie Shepherd.
F-150 restoration update: Painless wiring harness laid out and being routed. Body and interior next quarter. Bumpside patience required.
College application week ended well. Kai submitted three. Whatever comes next is hers to choose.
Green chile stew, my mother's frybread, and family. That is the whole recipe.
Four new pieces delivered to Turquoise Trail Gallery in Santa Fe for the holiday window. Cuff, two bolos, one belt buckle. Angela always finds the right customer for them.
Etsy holiday orders shipped. Twenty-six pieces out this month between the shop and the gallery. That is a good year.
Quiet Christmas morning. Coffee. Dog. Daughter still asleep. Nothing to fix, nothing to chase. Rare.
New year in the garage. Ford F-150 4x4, 1978, front leaf springs and steering rebuild. Slow work, satisfying work.
My brother Ben turned 46 yesterday. Drove up to Shiprock for a green chile burger. Elder brother privileges observed and unpaid.
Finished a simple cuff for my mother this weekend. Weight in the metal, no fuss. Seventy-five in March. Started planning early.
Schedule C for the studio filed. Small numbers, honest numbers. Coffee earned.
My mother turned 75 yesterday. Drove to Window Rock with a cake, gave her the cuff. She wore it. That is enough.
